refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—providing visibility and data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target is a performance constraint/acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation clarifies that the system must instrument and expose energy data; whether the target is met depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can continuously or periodically measure energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task units), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry. Verify accuracy of measurement against calibrated reference instruments. Confirm data is available for post-evaluation analysis over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: Measure total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: Measure or infer work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, task cycles completed) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: Compute and log the ratio of energy consumed to work output (energy_per_ton or equivalent metric).
FR-NGHE-003.4: Provide access to energy efficiency metrics to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, System Engineer) for evaluation and reporting.
refined_fr.note: The refined FR is more atomic than the original, but the four sub-FRs represent a complete decomposition of the measurement and reporting capability. Each sub-FR is now solution-neutral and testable independently.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

DP2: Deploy real-time energy metering on the power supply circuit and work-output sensors on the load/displacement mechanism; stream both signals to a local edge controller that computes and logs energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold is exceeded.

DP3: Capture energy consumption via utility billing records and cross-reference with operational logs of work completed; perform periodic (e.g., weekly/monthly) batch reconciliation to derive average energy_per_ton; report against target in summary dashboards.

DP4: Instrument energy input at the prime mover (engine/motor) using embedded power measurement; instrument work output via load cells or displacement transducers; implement a distributed measurement architecture where each subsystem reports its own energy_per_ton ratio locally; aggregate results at a supervisory layer for compliance verification.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

potential_sources: Measurement point independence: Energy and work-output sensors must be decoupled in their physical placement and signal conditioning to avoid cross-talk or shared failure modes.

Aggregation layer coupling: Centralized analytics creates a single point of dependency; mitigation is to ensure the analytics module has well-defined, versioned interfaces and can operate in degraded mode (e.g., buffering data locally if central system is unavailable).

Target threshold coupling: The comparison logic is tightly bound to the customer-defined E_target value; recommend parameterizing the threshold as a configuration input rather than hardcoding, to decouple the measurement logic from the acceptance criterion.

Reporting format coupling: Report generation should be decoupled from measurement computation; use an intermediate data model (e.g., standardized energy_per_ton records) to allow multiple reporting formats without re-engineering the core calculation.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 achieves functional independence by separating measurement (work and energy) from aggregation and comparison. The design avoids prescribing a specific sensor technology or communication protocol, preserving solution flexibility. Risk of coupling arises in the centralized analytics layer; recommend modular, stateless design for the aggregation and comparison functions to minimize tight dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured independently and reliably at the point of load transfer or displacement. Risk: If tonnage measurement is indirect (e.g., inferred from load cell readings under variable friction), systematic bias could skew energy_per_ton. Mitigation: Validate measurement accuracy against independent calibration standards before deployment.

Assumption: Energy input can be measured at a single point (e.g., main power feed or fuel consumption). Risk: If the system has multiple energy sources (e.g., grid + on-site generator, or hybrid prime movers), a single measurement point may not capture total energy. Mitigation: Explicitly define system boundary and ensure all energy inputs are instrumented.

Assumption: The evaluation period is long enough and operationally representative to yield statistically valid energy_per_ton ratios. Risk: Short evaluation windows or non-representative operating conditions (e.g., idle time, startup transients) could produce misleading results. Mitigation: Define evaluation period and operational baseline in the acceptance criteria; exclude non-productive phases from the calculation if appropriate.

Assumption: Centralized analytics layer has sufficient uptime and data integrity to support compliance reporting. Risk: Data loss, clock skew, or analytics downtime could invalidate reported results. Mitigation: Implement local buffering at measurement points, time-synchronization protocols, and audit trails for all reported values.

Assumption: Customer-defined E_target is stable and known at design time. Risk: If target changes mid-evaluation or is ambiguous (e.g., different targets for different work modes), the comparison logic may require rework. Mitigation: Establish a change control process for E_target and version all compliance reports with the target value used.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints that conflict with measurement instrumentation (e.g., sensor placement, data logging). Risk: Safety or operational restrictions could limit sensor accessibility or data collection frequency. Mitigation: Coordinate with safety and operations teams early; design measurement architecture to be non-intrusive and compliant with site policies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP1 to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Measurement subsystem: independent instrumentation of energy input and work output with local signal conditioning and buffering; (2) Aggregation subsystem: time-aligned collection and ratio computation with configurable evaluation windows; (3) Comparison subsystem: parameterized threshold logic and alert generation; (4) Reporting subsystem: standardized data export and compliance documentation. This modular decomposition reduces coupling and allows each subsystem to be designed, tested, and evolved independently. Additionally, add a requirement that all measurement points include calibration traceability and that the analytics layer maintains an immutable audit log of all energy_per_ton calculations and threshold comparisons for compliance verification.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized analytics layer that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, computes energy efficiency metrics (energy/ton), compares against compliance thresholds, and generates compliance reports.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) measurements from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules at independent measurement points.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits compliance violation alerts and threshold breach notifications when energy efficiency falls below target.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports data aggregation from distributed measurement points and report distribution to stakeholders.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is explicitly positioned as the centralized analytics layer. Aggregation, ratio computation, threshold comparison, and report generation are all core analytics functions. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (Vehicle/Teleoperation responsibility) and alert transmission (Safety Services responsibility) cross boundaries.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a data source, not an analytics platform. Placing aggregation and compliance logic here would scatter analytics across the vehicle domain, creating tight coupling with teleoperation and safety services. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor containment. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not an analytics engine. Embedding energy efficiency analytics here conflates operational control with compliance monitoring, reducing modularity and reusability.

Weak containment. While Safety Services may consume compliance alerts, it should not own the analytics computation. Safety Services is responsible for alert generation and response, not energy efficiency metric calculation. Mixing concerns reduces clarity of ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally an analytics and compliance monitoring function that requires a centralized, cross-system view of work output and energy consumption. NGHE Solution, as the designated analytics layer, is the natural and most cohesive boundary. It aggregates heterogeneous telemetry streams, applies domain logic (ratio computation, threshold comparison), and generates reports—all classic analytics responsibilities. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain data sources; Safety Services consumes alerts as outputs.
containment_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if measurement points are numerous or high-frequency. Mitigation: Design Telemetry interface with buffering and batching capabilities.
Risk: Threshold targets may vary by vehicle type, mission, or regulatory domain. Mitigation: Parameterize thresholds in NGHE Solution configuration, not hardcoded logic.
Risk: Report generation may require integration with external compliance systems. Mitigation: Define clear output contract for compliance reports; use Network Link for distribution.
Risk: Latency in aggregation and comparison may delay alert generation. Mitigation: Establish SLA for analytics pipeline; consider streaming vs. batch processing trade-offs.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution path: (1) independent measurement of work output (tonnage) and energy input (power), (2) centralized aggregation and ratio computation (energy/ton), (3) threshold comparison against customer targets, and (4) compliance report generation. The DP is logically sound and traces to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry interface design risk: If measurement points proliferate or emit high-frequency data, the Telemetry ingestion interface may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement buffering, batching, and rate-limiting at the interface boundary.

Threshold parameterization risk: If energy efficiency targets vary by vehicle type, mission profile, or regulatory jurisdiction, hardcoding thresholds in NGHE Solution logic will force code changes and reduce reusability. Mitigation: Externalize thresholds as configuration parameters; establish a configuration management interface.

Alert transmission coupling risk: SafetyAlert interface creates a dependency on Safety Services' alert handling contract. If Safety Services changes its alert schema or processing latency, NGHE Solution may be impacted. Mitigation: Define a versioned, stable alert contract; consider event-driven decoupling (publish-subscribe).

Report distribution latency risk: Network Link may introduce delays in compliance report delivery to stakeholders, potentially delaying corrective action. Mitigation: Establish SLA for report generation and transmission; monitor end-to-end latency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution exhibits excellent containment (0.92 score) for the analytics and compliance logic. The module boundary is well-defined: aggregation, ratio computation, threshold comparison, and report generation are all cohesive analytics responsibilities. Vehicle and Teleoperation correctly remain as data sources (not analytics owners), and Safety Services correctly consumes alerts (not owns compliance logic). The DP-to-IM mapping is clean with minimal diffusion (0.08). Ownership is unambiguous: NGHE Solution is the natural home for this DP.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly computable from the DP outputs (aggregated energy and tonnage measurements). NGHE Solution can emit energy_per_ton as a measurable metric and compare it against E_target. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of Vehicle and Teleoperation to emit accurate telemetry, (2) NGHE Solution to correctly aggregate and compute the ratio, and (3) test harness to validate the comparison logic. All are technically feasible within the proposed IM boundary.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the primary owner of energy efficiency analytics. Vehicle and Teleoperation are allocated as data sources (measurement responsibility). Safety Services is allocated as a consumer of compliance alerts. The allocation respects separation of concerns and avoids scattering the DP across multiple modules. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are external constraints that inform threshold targets and reporting requirements; they do not alter the IM allocation.

recommended_revisions: Revise Telemetry interface specification to include buffering, batching, and rate-limiting parameters to mitigate high-frequency measurement bottlenecks.

Externalize energy efficiency thresholds (E_target) as configuration parameters in NGHE Solution, keyed by vehicle type, mission profile, and regulatory domain. Establish a configuration management interface.
Define a versioned, stable SafetyAlert contract (schema, latency SLA, retry semantics) to decouple NGHE Solution from Safety Services implementation changes.
Establish an SLA for the analytics pipeline (telemetry ingestion → ratio computation → threshold comparison → report generation) to ensure timely alert generation and compliance reporting.
Document the evaluation period and aggregation window (e.g., per-shift, per-mission, per-week) in the DP to clarify how energy_per_ton is computed over time.
Add a Network Link specification for compliance report format, distribution frequency, and stakeholder endpoints to ensure reports are actionable and traceable.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass with high confidence (FR-DP: logically sound, DP-IM: excellent containment at 0.92, FR-IM: verification feasible). However, the recommended revisions and diffusion risks reveal that the current DP, while architecturally sound at the top level, depends on several critical sub-system capabilities that are not yet decomposed: (1) Telemetry interface robustness (buffering, batching, rate-limiting), (2) Threshold parameterization and configuration management, (3) SafetyAlert contract stability and versioning, (4) Analytics pipeline SLA and latency guarantees, and (5) Compliance report distribution and traceability. Given the high safety criticality of energy efficiency monitoring in autonomous heavy equipment, these sub-FRs must be explicitly derived and validated before implementation. Stopping here would leave architectural and operational risks unresolved. Deriving next-level sub-FRs will ensure that the DP is not only logically correct but also resilient, configurable, and operationally sound.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Ingest and buffer high-frequency telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation without loss or bottleneck, accommodating variable measurement rates and burst traffic.

FR1.2: Externalize and manage energy efficiency thresholds (E_target) as configuration parameters, keyed by vehicle type, mission profile, and regulatory jurisdiction, with versioning and audit trail.
FR1.3: Establish and enforce a versioned, stable SafetyAlert contract with defined schema, latency SLA, and retry semantics to decouple NGHE Solution from Safety Services implementation changes.
FR1.4: Guarantee end-to-end analytics pipeline latency (telemetry ingestion → ratio computation → threshold comparison → alert generation) within a defined SLA to ensure timely compliance response.
FR1.5: Generate, format, and distribute compliance reports to authorized stakeholders with traceability, audit trail, and delivery confirmation, respecting regulatory and operational requirements.
FR1.6: Aggregate energy and tonnage measurements over a defined evaluation period (per-shift, per-mission, per-week) with clear semantics for partial data, missing values, and time-window boundaries.

revise.why: The current DP (DP1) is logically sound but underspecifies critical operational and architectural concerns. Revise DP1 to explicitly incorporate: (1) telemetry ingestion strategy with buffering and rate-limiting, (2) threshold parameterization and configuration management interface, (3) SafetyAlert contract versioning and stability guarantees, (4) analytics pipeline SLA and latency monitoring, (5) compliance report format and distribution protocol, and (6) aggregation window and evaluation period semantics. This revision will elevate DP1 from a logical solution to an operationally complete design parameter that accounts for the sub-system dependencies identified in the diffusion risk analysis.

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation can emit accurate, timestamped telemetry at variable rates without loss.
Energy efficiency thresholds (E_target) are known and can be parameterized by vehicle type, mission profile, and regulatory domain.
Safety Services has a stable, versioned alert ingestion interface that can accept NGHE Solution compliance alerts.
Compliance reporting requirements are defined by regulatory and operational policies external to NGHE Solution.
The evaluation period for energy_per_ton computation (e.g., per-shift, per-mission, per-week) is operationally feasible and aligns with corrective action timelines.
Network Link has sufficient bandwidth and reliability to deliver compliance reports within acceptable latency bounds.
open_risks: Telemetry bottleneck: High-frequency measurement data may overwhelm the Telemetry ingestion interface if buffering and batching are not implemented. Mitigation: Derive FR1.1 to specify telemetry ingestion robustness.

Threshold brittleness: Hardcoded thresholds will force code changes and reduce reusability across vehicle types and regulatory domains. Mitigation: Derive FR1.2 to specify threshold parameterization and configuration management.
SafetyAlert coupling: Changes to Safety Services' alert schema or processing latency may break NGHE Solution. Mitigation: Derive FR1.3 to specify a stable, versioned alert contract.

Pipeline latency: Delays in telemetry ingestion, aggregation, or report transmission may delay corrective action, reducing safety effectiveness. Mitigation: Derive FR1.4 to specify end-to-end SLA and latency monitoring.

Report traceability: Compliance reports must be auditable and traceable to source measurements and threshold decisions for regulatory compliance and incident investigation. Mitigation: Derive FR1.5 to specify report format, distribution, and audit trail.

Aggregation semantics: Unclear evaluation period and handling of partial or missing data may lead to incorrect energy_per_ton computation. Mitigation: Derive FR1.6 to specify aggregation window and data quality rules.
evidence_needed: Telemetry emission rates and burst profiles from Vehicle and Teleoperation to inform buffering and batching strategy.
Energy efficiency threshold values (E_target) for each vehicle type, mission profile, and regulatory jurisdiction.
SafetyAlert schema and processing contract from Safety Services, including latency SLA and retry semantics.
Regulatory and operational requirements for compliance reporting (format, frequency, stakeholder endpoints, audit trail).
Operational timelines for corrective action (e.g., shift duration, mission duration) to inform evaluation period and SLA targets.
Network Link capacity and reliability metrics to validate compliance report delivery SLA.
Data quality requirements (e.g., handling of missing measurements, sensor failures, clock skew) to inform aggregation logic.
